title: Spec-folder save mutex reaps live owners (no pid-liveness gate, no heartbeat) — defeats single-writer guarantee

findingDetails.evidence: isStaleLock (lines 37-44) reaps purely on mtime age > LOCK_STALE_MS (5min, line 18); owner.json writes pid (lines 63-67) but it is never read back for liveness (repo-wide grep finds no process.kill/owner.json reader); lock dir mtime is set once at mkdir and never refreshed (no heartbeat); acquire reaps on isStaleLock alone (lines 74-77). The sibling lock generate-context.ts (commit 7c17b07e01 'save-lock liveness') already fixed this exact pattern with process.kill(ownerPid,0) liveness (lines 424-436), a utimesSync heartbeat (lines 448-461), and reap only on dead/unknown+stale (line 485); that commit did not touch spec-folder-mutex.ts (last changed 2026-05-14, a rename).
findingDetails.why: A save legitimately held past 5min (large/chunked embedding or a slow/retrying provider, all run inside fn() per atomic-index-memory.ts:320-411) has its lock reaped from a still-alive owner by a second process, which then proceeds — two concurrent indexers on the same spec folder, the precise TOCTOU the module exists to prevent. This epic added the multi-process triggers (daemon re-election; CLI front-door as a separate writer). The team's own remediation of the identical bug in the twin lock establishes it is a real, fix-worthy defect, not a false positive.
findingDetails.recommendation: Port the generate-context.ts pattern: parse owner.json pid, gate reap on process.kill(pid,0) (reap immediately on ESRCH/dead, fall back to the time threshold only when owner state is unreadable/unknown, never reap an alive pid), and run a utimesSync heartbeat on the lock dir for the lifetime of withSpecFolderLock's fn().
